turns.text: The written report from Litterly is in the record, and at my recollection of it, it is in the echo -- the report of the exhibits. The report of Litterly is found at --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: This is not the trial, isn't it?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: No this is the exhibits from the habeas corpus proceeding, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Habeas corpus.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: I think it's number six, Your Honor I'm looking at it now to verify it. Yes, it is petitioner's exhibit number six.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: This is what the prosecuting attorney knew?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Where -- which one of these items applies to the --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: The reproduction is not very good.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: No, it isn't Your Honor. I'm trying to Your Honor, I'm -- do you have one of your first? Yes, that's much better.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: I see some --

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: It's -- at the next to last page, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Can you read it?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Yes I can from the original or much better copy of the report than you have available.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: The pair of white jockey shorts?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Described as number 17?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Were examined by chemical means and found to be heavily stained with blood. Further serological examination include the blood was of human origin. It was probably of group A. Numerous hairs and fibers were herein to the shorts and were mounted in microscopic examination, a comparison of those hairs of pubic hairs 10 to 15 millimeters. Some similar characteristics, no definite opinions of whether or not the common origin is possible. One of the fibers found in the shorts was mounted and compared microscopically, samples of the fibers of the inner pant lining the color, size, etcetera.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: No mention of paint at all.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: No mention of paint, Your Honor.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Oh, what page is that on do you say?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: These are not numbered, Your Honor. This is exhibit number six, the next to the last page, Your Honor.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Well, this -- there's no mention here that some of the stains were not blood.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: No.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: No there is not, Your Honor.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Well, then where did the prosecuting attorney get his information that some of the stains were not blood?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: I do not know, Your Honor. All I know is that in the District Court he --

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Well, how about Mr. Litterly's testimony? He did testify that some of the stains were --

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Yes. He testified that he -- he did not testify that some of the stains were not. He testified that he knew that some of the stains were not blood.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Well, how about the testimony at the trial?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Litterly's testimony at the trial you're referring to now, Your Honor?

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Did he then say -- he didn't make any mention at all as to other stains, did he, in his testimony?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: No, Your Honor. Litterly's testimony at the trial appears in the brown colored Volume 4 at page 168.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Okay, thank you very much.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: It stated, “I examined and tested People's Exhibit 3 to determine the nature of the staining material thereon. That result of the first test was that this material upon the shorts is blood. I made a second examination it's as close as the blood of human origin and made a further examination which disclosed that the blood was of group A.” That was his testimony at that time.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, now that -- he made more -- testify?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Pardon me?

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: More fully did he, on the habeas corpus? Where was it that he finally conceded that some of the --

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: I'm not sure that he ever did. It was the state's attorney who conceded that he knew that some of the stains were not blood, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: That's the thing I referred to in form of my question.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Yes. Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: We submit that on this issue, Your Honors, the critical point is whether there was blood on these shorts. The petitioner testified that he --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Well, don't you think under a case like Napue against Illinois, the critical point is, even if there was blood on those shorts, a misrepresentation that the stains you could see there was blood when they knew it was -- if they knew it was paint.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: If there --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Isn't that the knowing use of concept of misrepresentation by the prosecution in a criminal case?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: If there is blood on these shorts, Your Honor, and the mere testimony by the prosecution only is that there are -- is blood in these shorts. And if they do refer to them as bloody shorts, if there is blood on them, I submit that even though there's also paint, there is no going beyond the truth, if you will, as indicated by Napue.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Well, as I understand it, today you can't find any blood on it?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: That is correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: On what's in there.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: However, the chemist for the prosecution did testify that he had no opinion today as to whether there was any blood on it or not.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Today the appearance of those and the --

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Except for one stain, Your Honor that is unidentifiable in origin.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, Mr. Michaels as you held those up, I don't -- it hardly seems an unspotted -- look at that.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: It --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: If the blood has now disappeared, there's certainly an awful lot of paint.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Yes there is, Your Honor.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Suppose the prosecutor had expressly represented to the jury that all of those spots were blood, would that have been your judgment, would that have been error under Illinois law?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: I would think not only under Illinois but under the decision of this Court it would have been, Your Honor.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: That's because it would've not been consummate with our ideas of a fair trial.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: So that what we have here is the variation in the sense that there was represented but there was blood on the shorts with no particularization of the exhibit, which was physically before the jury. It was not all blood but mostly paint as to the appearance to the naked eye now.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Yes sir. However, I do want to go back to the point I was making with respect to Mr. Justice Stewart's question. The petitioner did testify in his confession, admitted in his confession that these were his shorts. The critical issue --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Incidentally, where does this confession appear here, anywhere I can't --

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Yes, it is in the record, Your Honor on before -- it appears really in two places. One, Mr. Christensen who took the confession, testified as to what he said to him during the interrogation and then another time that Mr. Baudino read his confession into the record.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Where are the two places?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Mr. Christensen's statement begins on 128, I believe it is, Your Honor.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: That would be of Volume 1?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: This would be the trial transcript, Your Honor, the brown covered by --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Volume 4.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Volume 4.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: 128. Sorry to take your time but I haven't --

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: No, no. Then you have the -- we are looking now, Your Honor, for the -- where the confession.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Right, thank you very much.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Was there any evidence in the record of about how that paint got on those shorts?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: No, there's nothing at all, Your Honor, as far as I know.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: What was this man's occupation?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Would you excuse me? The confession Your Honor of the defendant was right under the record of page 144. Pardon me, Your Honor?

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: I asked, what was his occupation? As I recall it was evidence of him being a taxi driver.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Yes there was, Your Honor.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: He was not a painter.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: No, he was not, Your Honor.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I understood there was some evidence in one of the proceedings to the effect that these shorts did not fit the defendant. Is there anything from the prosecution to rebut that?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: The testimony was, Your Honor, that the -- they would've been tight and uncomfortable if he had worn them. There's nothing to rebut that, Your Honor.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Was there any rebuttal of that?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: No, there was not, Your Honor.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Anything to show that the confirmation of the man had changed in the years that he was either fatter or thinner at the time this testimony was taken and at the time of the crime?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: No, there was not, Your Honor.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: Perhaps I misunderstood. I thought you were starting to tell us at the time that I interrupted you to ask you where in the record the confession appeared. I thought you were trying to starting to tell us that in his confession, he admitted that those shorts were his?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Yes, he did Your Honor, in his confession. He later denied it.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: But he never made any confession.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Yes, but in his confession he admitted that the shorts were his. And so I submit that the relevant question is whether they did contain blood. If they did contain blood, this was the critical point. The presence of paint in addition to the blood, I respectfully submit, was immaterial.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Where did they get the shorts, from him?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: They found -- a policeman found them in a old abandoned apartment building which was referred to at that time as the Van Buren Flats. In his confession, he stated that he had discarded them in the Van Buren Flats, but if I remember the testimony correctly, the police had found it before his confession if I'm not mistaken on that point.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: I'm sorry must have read them -- perhaps I read it too quickly. This is the confession at page 145?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: 147, can you refer me to the admission that these particular shorts were his? I see that there is -- he states that he removed the shorts, but I don't find any --

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Perhaps it was in the statement of Mr. Christensen sir.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: If you don't find it there.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: I don't find it in the confession.

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: I found -- I find on a page 130 that he said he did this -- discard his shorts at Van Buren Flats, but that doesn't -- that's not the same as saying that those shorts are his.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: I agree with that, Your Honor. My recollection from the record is that he was shown the flats and he stated that they were -- I maybe in error on that. It was my recollection from reading the record, Your Honor.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Well he agreed that -- he conceded that they had been left at this place and they found these at that place.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: That's probably might be accountable for your error.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: That is --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: I find what you're referring to now, on page 132 just below the center of the page.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Michael, I gather the impression from reading the phrase that the state in introducing the confession and the presentation of its case told the jury or left the impression with the jury that he have had obtained the confession of the defendant and then had verified it through these things and that they had found these things as a result of the confession. And I gather the impression also that there had been evidence later produced to show that they had these things before the confession and verify them by this confession. Now, did the state deny the latter?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: As I remember that point, Your Honor, it's my understanding that the reading of the record that the items were found before the confession.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Now --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Was that at any time represented to the jury that these things were found afterwards as a result of that confession?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: The petitioner has claimed that they are, Your Honor. As I read that section that he referred to, I was not -- I thought it was ambiguous which way they were referring to it.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Would you have that citation recorded?

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Don't you think that's -- that might be important?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: If there were knowing misrepresentation to closing argument, I would say it would be very significant, Your Honor. If there was no evidence, however, brought out that he deliberately misstated anything, it is possible that a mistake could occur in -- by prosecution in closing argument which would not be deliberate mistake and would not amount to constitutional proportions.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: But neither that an important thing, it seems to me, whether these things were found and developed as a result of the plain confession that he made or whether they had these things and before the confession and did not report it to the --

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: The argument that you are referring to, Your Honor, is on page 375 of the volume printed by this Court, Volume 4, Your Honor. And it relates to the jacket of my -- attorney for the petitioner in form where he relates --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Where is that, the upper part of the volume?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: About the middle of the page I believe, Your Honor. They could not have known, but when they put it there to find, the jacket was found where he had said he had put it. Words are about the middle of the page, Your Honor.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: I beg your -- 375, I beg your pardon.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: That's the one they could not have known, but when they went there to find the jacket it was found --

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Yes sir.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: -- where he said he put it.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. We turn then Your Honors --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Now, did -- is -- which is true, that they did find this thing after the confession or before?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: My understanding of the record is that they found it before the confession, Your Honor.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: And that this, while it might not have been with intention to deceive, but was, untrue?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: If that is -- as I read that, Your Honor, I am not absolutely clear just what the meaning of it. If you interpret that as saying that we took his -- what he told us and went out and there we found it then it would be untrue, yes Your Honor.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: We turn then to the question of the advice to Mrs. Baxter that she had a right not to talk to the defense in this case. Now, Mrs. Baxter testified at the pardon and parole proceedings that she had been receiving many calls and was being bothered by the defense attorney and she was, in her words, perturbed. She called up the state's attorney and asked what the situation was. He told her in effect that, “You have a right not to talk to the defense if you want to.” This is corroborated by his own testimony at the District Court where he testified that he told other witnesses amongst her that by request that they had a right not to talk to the defense and they must make up their own mind whether they wanted to or not. Now, we submit that this statement is absolutely correct. Absent a subpoena to some particular hearing, every private individual has a right not only to talk not to the defense, but not to talk to the prosecution, absent an appropriate subpoena. We submit that there was absolutely no violation of any rights in respect to that statement.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I was wondering about her testimony when it was offered and when the Court was asked for permission to permit her testimony as a court witness. The prosecution said they would consent to it only if her unreliability was conceded. Now, why would that -- is that a normal thing?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Let me correct that statement. Just -- my understanding, Your Honor is with that the Court said that rather than the prosecution. The Court said, “If you will state to me that she is unreliable in some way, I will allow you to call her as a court's witness. If you do not so state then I will give you great latitude in asking leading questions and various examinations you want, but I will not call her as a court witness.” They then refused that offer -- they refused the Court's offer to have called her and have this great leeway in question -- with respect to leading questions. I assume they testified because they did not want to be bound by the testimony of someone whom they did not know who was going to testify.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Is that a test of whether a person -- the defendant should be entitled to call a witness as the Court's witness that he must admit that there's some unreliability?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: I think it is -- the question is pretty much within the discretion of the Court, Your Honor. And I don't think it's subject to review for abuse in the same way as the scientific evidence was as I referred earlier to the question of Mr. Justice Fortas. I think that if the Court -- if you can show some reason to the Court why he should be called as the Court's witness, that he pretty well usually is. The Court, in exercise of its discretion in this case, merely believed that they had not shown sufficient grounds to exercise his discretion in that regard. We turn then to the question of the unreliable witness, Betty Baldwin, not Betty Baldwin-Curry. The prosecution -- the petitioner in this case contends that the prosecution was in error for calling that witness when they knew that she was unreliable. We submit, Your Honors, that there is absolutely no constitutional requirement that prohibits the calling of a witness, who in the past has not been believed if, and this is the big if, they do believe her testimony on this occasion. Now, on this occasion, the evidence is rather complete that at least two people so testified that they fully believed her on this occasion. On that basis, they called her and we believe that it is a proper to call a witness whose testimony you believe even though at some past occasion you may not have believed this witness' testimony.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Which one would they believe? Which part of her testimony would they believe, for in the Court, she said she had known him for two years and prior to that time she had told the police that she knew him for five weeks?

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Well, there is another factor in that regard, Your Honor. When -- if the she had told the police that she knew him at the time of the crime for five weeks. Now, this question was asked at the trial which was ten months after the crime. How long have you known him, not how long had you known him Your Honor, not how long had you known him at the time of the crime, but how long have you known him, which means from the present past. And I submit, Your Honor, that the correct answer to that would have been 11 months, because she had known him at the time that question was asked for 11 months. Now, I submit Your Honor, that to allow that to go unrefuted is absolutely immaterial. There is no materiality at all in this record to the significance of whether that she had known him 11 months or two years. The defendant states there is some materiality --

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Do you have the citation to that, but don't take the time to look. If you don't know, don't bother.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: You mean as to the -- where as to the formal questioned asked?

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: As to whether they said, “How long have you known him?”

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Yes I have it right here, Your Honor, it's in Volume 2, -- Volume 2 page 702 at the top of the page. At the very top of the page, Your Honor, it refers “Do you know the defendant Lloyd Eldon Miller?” She said, “Yes sir.” “How long have you known him?” “Approximately two years.” The principle -- there is concluding that thought Your Honor also if there's a contention under Napue versus Illinois that they allowed knowing testimony to go in the record as they did not contradict that statement. There is absolutely no evidence in this record that they were aware of the statement was made. Now, there was the fact that that statement had been made to the prosecution, but there was no evidence that they had gone down that statement and checked every one of her answers against them, I'm sure they knew it in substance. But this was such a, I submit, immaterial point that they probably didn't even realize that she was violating the statement that they had given, or at least there's no evidence in the record that they had, Your Honor.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Michael, may I ask you concerning this witness if it is the fact that they have concluded before this proceeding that this witness had made a false accusation against another person for a crime that she said he had confessed to her but was not true. And then also that she had made another one which was under investigation and which the experts found that it was inconclusive but they believed that she was not telling the truth and that the county authorities who prosecuted this case suggested that they discontinue the investigation of the truth of that statement until this case was tried so she could be a witness here.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: Your Honor, what you say is very -- is in substance. Now, I know of only one prior occasion that she had made a story about another crime that was this narcotics story. With respect to that statement, she was given a lie detector test to check whether it was right. The result was inconclusive. They said that they found -- they did not believe her, although the test was inconclusive and that they were going to drop the matter. The only other instance that I can think of which talks to their -- to the unreliability relates to the statement that Mr. Malmgren, the attorney for the petitioner had tried to coerce this witness. Now, as far as I read the record in this case, those claims have never been disproving, Your Honor.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: I understood that there was an investigation of that, and the authorities indicated that they thought that was not true, but the people who prosecuted this case suggested that the investigation be discontinued until after the trial of this case.

turns.name: RICHARD A. MICHAEL
turns.text: That was -- the statement that they believe was not true of hers related to the narcotics story, Your Honor. There was no evidence one way or another whether they believed the story with respect to the Attorney Malmgren. However, they did -- the statement -- the prosecution did say, “Let's not get that question out of this case so there'll be no question of prejudice,” you know, to the defendant by the fact that his attorney made the some question of improper statements to a prosecution witness. So that was how it all advanced and was either the last statement in the record on that Your Honor is that it either still pending or is been dismissed. I assume it has been dismissed, but there has never been a hearing on it. It has never been proved conclusively one way or another whether that allegation was correct or not. Thank you, Your Honors.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Mr. Lassers.

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court. I believe that everything that we desire to say has been said, unless the Court has questions, I have nothing further.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Very well.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Can I ask you one last question?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: Certainly.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: In your petition for habeas corpus in the District Court, did you rely on the ground of coerced confession?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. My recollection is that we did. Yes, we did rely on that.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Well, did the District Court rule on it or not?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: He ruled that it was not coerced, Mr. Justice White.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Did the Court of Appeals rule?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: I think they did.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: But at least you -- this is not the first time you've raised the issue of coerced confession in this Court, isn't it?

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: In this Court?

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: You've raised it below, on the preserved two points of the --

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: Oh, yes!

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: Very well.

turns.name: WILLARD J. LASSERS
turns.text: Thank you.
